Part B.key_evidence: Difference image shows a strong dipole/yin-yang residual at the center with adjacent positive and negative lobes, characteristic of a subtraction artifact. Science and Reference both show similar bright sources at/near the center, suggesting the residual is from PSF/astrometric mismatch rather than a true new source. The nearest PS1 source is very bright (sgmag1=14.05, srmag1=14.10, sgscore1=0.99) at 5.8 arcsec, indicating a bright star in the field that can corrupt subtractions. ndethist=1 with ncovhist=978 means this is a first-time detection at a heavily monitored location.
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: Most likely bogus: the difference panel exhibits a clear dipole pattern next to a bright stellar field, typical of imperfect image subtraction near bright stars. The high sgscore1 and bright PS1 magnitudes of the nearby source support contamination from a bright star. With only 1 detection in nearly 1000 visits, sustained variability is unlikely.
Part B.alternative_analysis: An alternative is a genuine variable star, since the field is crowded and a stellar source could brighten in g-band. However, the dipole morphology in the difference image and the absence of a clean positive PSF residual argue against a clean astrophysical detection.
